99:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, I expect the Sessions page to reflect reality — if I ran `ddx work` today, today's sessions appear within seconds. And I expect the feed not to silently break again the next time the agent execution path is refactored.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Session-index source of truth.** A single writer (invoked from `service.Execute` or a thin wrapper that every production agent-execution path calls) appends one line per completed agent invocation to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`. Fields are pointer-only: id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath (relative), nativeLogRef (relative). No prompt or response bodies embedded.

2. **Single-call-site guarantee.** Unit test enumerates every production code path that executes an agent (execute-bead, execute-loop, `ddx agent run`, quorum, replay if still present) and asserts each appends exactly one index row. If a new path is added later without wiring this up, the test fails.

3. **Visibility restored.** GraphQL `sessions` query reads `sessions.index.jsonl`. Running `ddx work` (or any agent-execution path) against a fixture project causes the new session to appear in a GraphQL query within 2 seconds of completion. E2E test demonstrates this.

4. **Body lazy-load.** A new resolver `session(id: ID!) { prompt response stderr }` loads the heavy fields on demand from the execution bundle (`.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/`) or the agent-log path, without reading every session in the index.

5. **Legacy migration.** A one-shot command (e.g. `ddx agent log reindex`) seeds the index from the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` (397 MB corpus on the current repo), preserving historical sessions. Integration test uses a fixture `sessions.jsonl` and asserts the index has one row per legacy entry with all pointer fields populated.

6. **Deprecated-file behavior.** After migration, the legacy `sessions.jsonl` is not read by the server. `ddx agent usage` and related CLI commands (`cli/cmd/agent_usage.go:197,246,348`, `cli/cmd/status.go:179`) are updated to read from the index too, verified by their existing tests.

7. **Perf sanity.** The GraphQL `sessions` list resolves in ≤100ms p95 on a fixture with 10,000 index entries; current behavior times out or OOMs. Benchmark captured in bead notes as baseline + after-fix.

8. **UI note.** If there is a gap in the index (e.g. the 2026-04-19 → 2026-04-22 gap on the current repo, assuming backfill from executions/agent-logs is not feasible), the Sessions page surfaces a single-line banner "No sessions recorded between X and Y" so the gap is not silently invisible. If backfill is feasible, do it instead and skip the banner.

9. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds a fixture project with `.ddx/executions/` bundles from both before and after a simulated gap.
   - Runs the migration command.
   - Opens `/sessions`, asserts the most recent session timestamp matches the latest bundle, asserts a clicked row lazy-loads prompt/response from the bundle rather than from an embedded payload.
   - Asserts the feed updates live (within 2s) after a new simulated session is written.","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:22:41.990684692Z","description":"## Observed

The Sessions page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../sessions` shows nothing newer than **2026-04-19**, three days ago. ddx is definitely still running — `.ddx/executions/` contains bundles from 2026-04-22 (today), `agent-s-*.jsonl` from 2026-04-21, `agent-loop-*.jsonl` from 2026-04-20. The UI simply cannot see them.

## Root cause

The Sessions GraphQL resolver reads exclusively from `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:504-516`). That file stopped being appended on 2026-04-19 19:46.

The only writer is `Runner.logSession` (`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:847-914`). It is called from four sites today:

- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:229` (Runner.Run — legacy)
- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:216`
- `cli/internal/agent/claude_stream.go:516`
- `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go:206`

Production execute-bead / execute-loop no longer goes through any of them. The Runner-to-service refactor series (`5b933103`, `280f58e0`, `74eceda9`, `7cc1eb58`, `e05c785e`, `9f79f037`) routed the common path through `service.Execute` directly and did not carry forward the sessions.jsonl append. The last sessions.jsonl write lines up with the day that refactor set became default.

## Secondary issue (same fix should address)

`sessions.jsonl` is 397 MB for only 895 entries because each entry embeds the full `result.Output` (see `SessionEntry.Response` at `runner.go:879`). Growth is unbounded, there is no rotation, and `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` (`state_graphql.go:476-490`) reads the whole file, parses every entry, and sorts in memory on every query. Even if we just restore the writer, the feed is fragile and will become a perf problem again quickly — relevant to `ddx-ad0db8fd` (bead detail was also O(N) scan).

## Proposed direction

Do not simply paper over by re-plumbing `logSession` into `service.Execute`. The right fix is:

1. **Decide the source of truth for "a session".** Current candidates on disk:
   - `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/{manifest.json,prompt.md,result.json}` — already written per execute-bead attempt.
   - `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` and `agent-s-*.jsonl` — per-run streams from the agent harness.
   - Bead evidence events (kind:cost already written per attempt, see `execute_bead.go:492`).
   A session record should be derivable from a combination of execution bundles + agent-log streams, not from a separately-appended aggregate. The aggregate should be an **index**, not a **copy**.

2. **Introduce a lightweight index.** A thin `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl` containing `{id, projectID, beadID, harness, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, bundlePath}` — pointer fields only, no embedded response bodies. Existing `sessions.jsonl` is deprecated to a read-only legacy file during a grace period.

3. **Write the index from `service.Execute`** (or a wrapper the execute-bead / run / execute-loop paths all share). One place to touch. Unit test guards that every production code path that executes an agent appends exactly one index row.

4. **GraphQL reads the index, streams bodies on demand.** `sessions { edges { node { ... } } }` reads the index. A new `session(id: ID!) { prompt, response, stderr }` lazy-loads the heavy fields from the execution bundle or agent-log file when the user expands a row.

5. **Legacy sessions.jsonl compatibility.** The index can be seeded once from the existing 397 MB file so historical data is not lost, then the old file is not read again. One-shot migration command (`ddx agent log reindex` or similar).

## Out of scope

- Replaying or regenerating sessions for the 2026-04-19 → 2026-04-22 gap. If the evidence exists in executions/agent-logs we can backfill the index; if not, accept the gap and cap it with a dated marker in the UI.
- A full telemetry rework. base_rev=7875da7c9b0c4215cb67e005ae047e2c411a3688","created_at":"2026-04-23T01:06:16.391796646Z","kind":"execute-bead","source":"ddx agent execute-loop","summary":"review_block"}],"execute-loop-last-detail":"escalation exhausted: unsupported model "minimax/minimax-m2.7" for harness "gemini"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","feature":"FEAT-010","id":"ddx-2ceb02fa","issue_type":"bug","labels":["feat-008","feat-010","feat-006","perf","observability"],"notes":"## 2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index

Per user feedback: a single `sessions.index.jsonl` just replays the same problem at smaller scale. The aggregate needs to be split.

### Revised storage shape

- Directory: `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` (per-project, under each registered project's `.ddx`).
- Inside: **monthly shards** named `sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`.
- Each shard is pointer-only, same fields as the previously-proposed index (id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath, nativeLogRef).
- Writer resolves the target shard from `time.Now().UTC()` at append time and creates the shard file lazily on first write of the month.
- Heavy bodies (prompt, response, stderr) stay where they already live — `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` and agent-log streams — and are lazy-loaded by the `session(id:)` resolver.

### Why monthly, not daily / size-based / SQLite

- **Daily** — produces tens of thousands of files over the project lifetime; ReadDir becomes the cost.
- **Size-based** — requires coordinating rotation with the append; race-prone and hard to test.
- **SQLite** — overkill for first pass; revisit if a single monthly shard grows past tens of MB.
- **Monthly** — ≤12 shards/year, human-readable filenames, date-range queries map trivially to shard selection, bounded shard size in practice.

### Reader behavior

- Default Sessions query loads the **current + previous shard** (covers "recent activity" for any current date).
- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.
- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.
- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.

### Legacy migration

- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.
- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.
- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).

### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)

1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.

5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.

6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.

7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.

(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)

### Scope clarifier

This amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.
## 2026-04-23 — Implementation benchmark and evidence

Baseline: `go test ./internal/agent -run '^$' -bench 'Benchmark(ReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow|LegacySessionsJSONLFullScan)$' -benchtime=1x` measured legacy single-file full scan/sort of 120k legacy-shaped rows with 2KB embedded responses at `BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan-12 1 1731705957 ns/op`.

After fix: the same command measured the sharded default-window reader at `BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow-12 1 8103661 ns/op` for 12 monthly shards x 10k rows each (120k total), with the default query reading only the current + previous shard window. Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.

Acceptance evidence: `TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards`, `TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange`, `TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes`, `TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow`, `TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter`, `TestGraphQLAgentSessions`, `TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile`, existing agent usage/status/processmetrics tests, and `e2e/sessions.spec.ts` cover writer sharding, bounded reads, migration, lazy body loading, deprecated-file read removal, UI gap banner, and live refresh.

REVIEW:BLOCK

Two options:
A. Keep in DDx — these are bead-driven orchestration concerns (per-attempt evidence appending, adaptive min-tier promotion based on cheap-tier success rate). They wrap the per-call service.Execute with retry-on-tier-mismatch. Not LLM routing per se; bead routing.
B. Move to agent — package as a stall-style policy on ExecuteRequest (StallPolicy already enforces some retry; could extend to TierPolicy).

Recommendation: A. Tier escalation is bead-driven workflow logic (consults DDx-side adaptive min-tier from execution evidence), not per-LLM-call concern. Should stay in DDx as a thin orchestration wrapper around service.Execute.

Outcome of this bead: tier_escalation.go stays in DDx; the file-delete bead ddx-1bf6c0df is RE-SCOPED to "ensure tier_escalation.go does not depend on routing.go internals" rather than "delete the file." Update bead notes accordingly.","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-19T03:10:33.016266096Z","id":"ddx-378d381f","issue_type":"decision","labels":["phase:design","area:agent","kind:scope"],"notes":"DECISION: Tier-escalation stays in DDx as orchestration logic.

Existing execution evidence continues to satisfy auditability: either still tracked but excluded from review diffs, or moved to a dedicated ref / ignored store per the chosen fix. `ddx agent metrics` and `ddx bead metrics` still surface per-attempt usage/tokens/cost from the evidence bundle after the fix.","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T00:13:24Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1551897","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:08:23.667089738Z","description":"## Summary

On retry, DDx synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt whose <diff> section is the output of `git show <resultRev>`. When the prior attempt's landing sequence appended an evidence commit of the form `chore: add execution evidence [<attempt_id>]` that added `.ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/embedded/agent-*.jsonl` (full per-event NDJSON session log) as a tracked file, `resultRev` is that evidence commit's SHA (because `landEvidence` sets `result.NewTip = sha` and the orchestrator copies `land.NewTip` into `res.ResultRev`). `git show` of the evidence commit emits the entire session log diff with a `+` prefix per line. Session logs are 5-50k lines; the retry prompt exceeds every public context window.

## Category / severity / surface

- Category: correctness / resource-exhaustion
- Severity: high
- Surface: `ddx agent execute-bead` retry path triggered by `kind:review-malfunction` (and any other flow that re-synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt from a git diff/show since base_rev).

## Full bug description (verbatim)

On retry, DDx synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt whose <diff> section is the raw `git diff <base_rev>..<result_rev>` of the prior attempt. The prior attempt's landing sequence includes an evidence commit of the form:

    chore: add execution evidence [<attempt_id>]

that adds `.ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/embedded/agent-*.jsonl` — the full per-event NDJSON session log of the prior attempt — as a tracked file. Because that file is *added* in the diff range, the diff includes it in full with a `+` prefix on every line. Session logs are routinely 5-50k lines. One reasonable fix iteration is enough to push the retry prompt past 1-2 million tokens, which no currently supported model can accept — the provider streams back an n_keep > n_ctx error and the retry crashes without a request round-trip.

This is a self-poisoning loop: the "evidence" DDx commits to make attempts auditable is the exact artifact that makes the next attempt undeliverable.

## Reproduction (observed live 2026-04-17 in nexiq)

1. Bead `hx-dd4ab35f` claimed by execute-loop worker (harness=agent, model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b, provider=vidar).
2. Attempt `20260417T211600-d6a7dee6` succeeded and landed two commits: `cb5e733` (implementation + regression test [hx-dd4ab35f]) and `9a006dd` (chore: add execution evidence [20260417T211600-], adds `.ddx/executions/20260417T211600-d6a7dee6/embedded/agent-1776460560227794836.jsonl`, +20,623 lines).
3. Post-merge review emitted `BLOCK` without a rationale; DDx recorded `kind:review-malfunction` and reopened the bead for retry.
4. Retry `20260417T212928-27f9054a` synthesized `.ddx/executions/20260417T212928-27f9054a/prompt.md` of 6,307,997 bytes; `<bead-review>` block = 6,307,969 bytes; 99.9998% is the diff of the prior evidence commit, 99%+ of that diff is the session log file. Rest of <bead-review> (title/description/acceptance/labels/governing) is ~28 bytes.
5. lmstudio responded: "n_keep: 2,274,074 >= n_ctx: 196,608". Worker recorded failed attempt with zero usage. Same pattern on vidar (n_keep: 1,169,147) and bragi (n_keep: 2,274,074), all running qwen3.6-35b-a3b at max_context_length 262144. Provider/model is irrelevant — prompt exceeds every public context window.

## DDx code-path verification (Claude investigation 2026-04-17)

Relevant files and line ranges in /Users/erik/Projects/ddx:

- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:553-561` — `DefaultBeadReviewer.gitShow(rev)` runs `git -C <projectRoot> show <rev>` with no pathspec. Output is injected raw into `<diff>` by `BuildReviewPrompt` at `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:383-384`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:427-431` — reviewer fetches `diff := r.gitShow(resultRev)` then builds the prompt.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go:540-555` (`landEvidence`) — creates `chore: add execution evidence [<short>]` and sets `result.NewTip = sha`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go:428-434` — `RealLandingGitOps.StageDir` runs `git add -- <relPath>` on the entire `.ddx/executions/<attempt>/` directory with no exclusions for `embedded/`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go:250-251` — after Land, `res.ResultRev = land.NewTip`, so `ResultRev` ends up pointing at the evidence commit. The reviewer then runs `git show <evidence-commit>`, which is literally the dump of the session log.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:1135-1164` (`VerifyCleanWorktree`) — also stages the evidence dir via `git add -- <evidenceDir>` and commits `chore: add execution evidence [<short>]`; same no-exclusion pattern.
- `cli/cmd/bead_review.go:162-171` (`beadReviewGitShow`) — mirror of the same bug in the standalone `ddx bead review` command.

Confirmation the bug reporter's claim about `:(exclude).ddx/executions/*/embedded`:
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:269-283` (`SynthesizeCommit`) — the exclusion pathspec IS present here; the bug is that the evidence-landing commit path (`StageDir` + `VerifyCleanWorktree`) and the review path (`git show <rev>`) do not share this exclusion.
- `cli/cmd/init.go:19-46` — the gitignore rules DO include `.ddx/executions/*/embedded/`, but `git add -- <dir>` on pre-existing or explicitly-added paths can still stage, and older workspaces may pre-date this gitignore (matches nexiq's scenario where the evidence commit landed the session log).

## Deviation from bug description (minor)

The bug text describes the diff as `git diff <base_rev>..<result_rev>`. The code actually does `git show <resultRev>` (single-rev show). The self-poisoning mechanism is functionally identical: when `resultRev` points at the evidence commit (`landEvidence` + `res.ResultRev = land.NewTip`), `git show` emits the entire `+`-prefixed session log. All suggested fixes still apply.

## Suggested fixes (ascending scope)

1. Exclude execution-evidence paths from bead-review diffs by passing a pathspec to the `git show`/`git diff` call in the prompt builder: `-- . ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/embedded/*' ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/prompt.md' ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/usage.json'`. The same exclusion already exists at `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:269-283` for `SynthesizeCommit`; replicate it in `DefaultBeadReviewer.gitShow` (`execute_bead_review.go:553-561`), `beadReviewGitShow` (`cli/cmd/bead_review.go:162-171`), `landEvidence` → `StageDir` (`execute_bead_land.go:428-434,540-555`), and `VerifyCleanWorktree` (`execute_bead.go:1135-1164`).
2. Substitute, don't reproduce. When a tracked-file addition exceeds a byte/line budget in the diff, replace it with a one-line stub such as: `+++ b/.ddx/executions/.../agent-*.jsonl  (+20,623 lines of session evidence — omitted from review prompt)`.
3. Stop committing raw session logs as tracked evidence. Move `.ddx/executions/*/embedded/*.jsonl` to a gitignored store, or a separate `refs/ddx/evidence/*` ref analogous to `refs/ddx/iterations/*`.
4. Cap the <bead-review> prompt size with a hard budget (e.g., 150k tokens). Elide to summary + log warning when exceeded.

(1) is the minimal surgical fix; (3) is the correct long-term fix.

## Evidence in nexiq repo

- Bloated retry prompt: `.ddx/executions/20260417T212928-27f9054a/prompt.md` (6,307,997 bytes; <bead-review> body = 6,307,969 bytes).
- Successful prior attempt: `.ddx/executions/20260417T211600-d6a7dee6/` (landed cb5e733 + 9a006dd).
- Failing agent session: `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-1776460487823541600.jsonl` (session.end payload has the n_keep/n_ctx error verbatim).
- Bead event trail: `ddx bead show hx-dd4ab35f` — routing, then `kind:review-malfunction summary:BLOCK without rationale`, then `kind:execute-bead summary:review_malfunction`.","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-18T00:13:24.428420423Z","id":"ddx-39e27896","issue_type":"bug","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:bug","area:agent","area:execute-bead","area:prompt-synthesis"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"FEAT-006","status":"closed","title":"execute-bead retry prompt explodes when prior attempt committed session-log evidence","updated_at":"2026-04-18T00:29:15.347462806Z"}

The autorouting epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on any of them for its exit gate; they are Phase 2 tracked separately.","closing_commit_sha":"abeea29135139fae380daa51b3bb6df51e83395d","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:14:30.750496873Z","description":"## Problem (filed per codex + opus fresh-eyes review 2026-04-17)

Both reviews flagged the same missing concept: routing currently scores candidates on cost class, quota, and historical success rate — but does NOT gate on whether the resolved route can actually execute the requested work. Concrete failure modes this lets through:

1. **Context-window overflow.** Bead prompt is 80k tokens. Fuzzy match picks a 32k-context model on the pinned provider. Routing 'succeeds'. Dispatch fails mid-stream with a provider 400 or truncated output.

2. **Missing tool-calling support.** Some omlx flavors or older local servers don't implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Routing picks them via fuzzy match. Dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' or silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop can't use.

3. **Missing structured-output support.** Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / tool-use-required. If the bead prompt relies on it, routing-level blindness means the failure only surfaces at dispatch.

4. **Effort / permissions.** --effort high or --permissions unrestricted may not be honored by every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and fail.

Codex: 'routing currently scores mostly on cost/quota/history, not whether a provider/model can satisfy tool calling, effort controls, permissions, or context-window needs.'
Opus: 'If the bead prompt is 80k tokens and the fuzzy-matched model is 32k, routing succeeds and dispatch fails mid-stream. Omlx flavor may not support tools at all.'

## Out of scope for this bead

This bead tracks the CONCEPT as a sibling of the main autorouting epic, but a full capability-constraint scoring system is a larger change than the current Phase-1 scope. Per opus: 'Scope this out of the epic explicitly (don't expand), but add a sibling bead so it's tracked.'

Split out into sub-scope bites is TBD:

1. **Context-window gating** — smallest useful slice. RouteRequest gains an optional EstimatedPromptTokens field; evaluateCandidate rejects (or penalizes) candidates whose concrete_model's known context window is below that. Uses v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits + catalog ContextWindow fallback.

2. **Tool-calling support gating** — needs per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata. Likely populated from discovery (/v1/models/status) + hand-curated capability table for harnesses.

3. **Effort / permissions gating** — mostly already in place for harnesses (Harness.EffortFlag checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable) but provider-specific gating (e.g., 'openrouter doesn't honor effort on non-reasoning models') is not.

4. **Structured-output support** — probably lowest priority; most reasoning-tool-capable models also do structured output.

This bead's AC is to file the sub-beads (smaller, focused) and close THIS bead once the sub-beads exist. The epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on those sub-beads for its exit gate — context-window etc. are a Phase 2 concern. The epic is about correct resolution; capability gating is about preventing resolved-but-unrunnable routes.

## Files touched (by sub-beads, not this one)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go — CandidatePlan capability fields, RouteRequest capability hints
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — capability checks in rejectIfNotViable + scoring
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go — ContextWindow, supports_tools, supports_structured_output per ModelEntry/Surface","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"claude","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-18T06:56:44.421900898Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=claude"},{"actor":"erik","body":"Bead ddx-4817edfd was a tracker-only bead: its AC was to file four sub-beads

rationale: Bead ddx-4817edfd was a tracker-only bead: its AC was to file four sub-beads
and close the parent as 'decomposed'. All five AC items are satisfied via
`ddx bead create` and `ddx bead update --status closed`, but those mutations
land in the main repo's bead store at /Users/erik/Projects/ddx/.ddx/beads.jsonl
(outside this worktree's git tree), so `git status` in this worktree shows no
intentional changes to commit. Hence this rationale instead of an empty/no-op
commit.

(a) What is done

1. Four sub-beads filed with parent=ddx-4817edfd:

   - .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-f5cec42a (P2)
     "routing: context-window gating in evaluateCandidate"
     Smallest useful slice: RouteRequest.EstimatedPromptTokens, reject
     candidates whose known context window is below it; unknown passes with
     warning. Caller-side estimate via char/4 in execute-bead. Files:
     cli/internal/agent/{types,routing,catalog}.go and the bead-prompt
     assembly site.

   - .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-854cb8a7 (P2)
     "routing: tool-calling support gating with per-flavor capability metadata"
     SupportsTools tri-state on ModelEntry/Surface, curated capability table
     in a new cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go, discovery enrichment via
     /v1/models/status, RouteRequest.RequiresTools (execute-bead sets true),
     rejection in evaluateCandidate.

   - .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-bb230ad5 (P3)
     "routing: effort/permissions gating per provider+model (not just per harness)"
     SupportsEffort and SupportsPermission on the curated table; reject when
     RouteRequest names a value not honored by the (provider, model) combo.
     Documents the openrouter-non-reasoning-model exception inline.

   - .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-22cadb21 (P4)
     "routing: structured-output (JSON-mode) gating — likely incidental to tool-calling"
     Decision-gate-first bead: verify the SupportsTools => SupportsStructuredOutput
     hypothesis on currently-routed providers; if true, close as 'covered by
     tool-calling gating'; otherwise add SupportsStructuredOutput parallel
     to the tool-calling bead.

2. Parent ddx-4817edfd updated to status=closed with notes referencing each
   sub-bead by ID and reaffirming that the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641 does
   NOT depend on these for its exit gate (Phase 2 work, tracked separately).

AC walk-through:
  AC1: context-window sub-bead exists and is prioritized -> ddx-...-f5cec42a (P2). PASS
  AC2: tool-calling sub-bead exists -> ddx-...-854cb8a7 (P2). PASS
  AC3: effort/permissions sub-bead exists -> ddx-...-bb230ad5 (P3). PASS
  AC4: structured-output sub-bead exists -> ddx-...-22cadb21 (P4). PASS
  AC5: this bead closed as 'decomposed - see sub-beads' -> ddx-4817edfd status=closed
       with notes pointing at the four sub-bead IDs; no dependency edge added
       to ddx-2d974641 from any of them. PASS

(b) What is blocking

Nothing functional. One cosmetic snag worth noting for a separate ddx-side
bead: `ddx bead create` invoked from inside this execution worktree generates
IDs whose prefix is the worktree directory's basename
(.execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-XXXX) instead of the
conventional 'ddx-XXXX' namespace. The beads themselves are correct (right
parent, right fields, queryable by `ddx bead show <full-id>`); only the IDs
are ugly. This is an upstream wart in DDx's bead-ID generation that probably
derives the prefix from cwd basename rather than from the configured
namespace; it should be filed as a separate ddx-CLI bead, not patched as
part of this work.

(c) What a follow-up attempt would need

None for THIS bead — the AC is met. The four sub-beads themselves are
follow-up work owned by future execute-bead runs (or hand work) and will be
queued via `ddx bead ready` when the autorouting epic moves into Phase 2.

221:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator looking at the DDx web UI, I want to understand at a glance what Workers are vs Sessions, and I want to stop a misbehaving worker or start a new queue drain without dropping to a terminal, so the UI is a real operator surface instead of a read-only status page.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Copy + IA.** Workers page header includes a one-sentence description distinguishing it from Sessions. Sessions page likewise. Each links to the other. Playwright asserts both descriptions are present and the cross-links navigate.

2. **Worker → Sessions navigation.** On worker detail, a "Sessions" section lists sessions produced by that worker (join on `session.workerId`). Empty state reads "No sessions recorded yet." If session records do not yet carry a worker ID, that gap is filled as part of this bead and backfilled for existing sessions in the current run (no historical backfill required).

3. **Session → Worker backlink.** Expanded session row shows worker ID as a clickable link when present.

4. **Stop worker.** Each row with `state == 'running'` shows a Stop button. Clicking prompts for confirm, then fires a GraphQL mutation that stops the worker. UI updates within 2s via the existing subscription/poll path. Integration test asserts: button visibility is gated on state, confirm dialog is required, and mutation invokes the same code path as `ddx agent workers stop`.

5. **Start worker.** A "Start worker" button in the Workers page header opens a form (harness/profile, effort, optional label filter). Submitting fires a `startWorker` mutation. On success the new worker appears in the list within 2s. Form validation prevents empty submissions. The code path reuses `ddx work` / execute-loop internals — not a separate implementation.

6. **Audit trail.** Every lifecycle action (start, stop, cancel) is recorded as a timestamped event visible on the worker detail page, including the actor identifier the server has for the requester.

7. **No regressions.** Existing live log streaming, recent events, and phase overrides continue to work. Playwright smoke of the worker detail page passes.

8. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Navigates to Workers page, asserts header description.
   - Clicks "Start worker", fills form, submits, asserts new row appears.
   - Clicks Stop on that row, confirms, asserts row state transitions to `stopped`.
   - Clicks into the stopped worker, asserts the Sessions section renders (or the empty state).
   - Asserts the "Sessions" link in the worker page header navigates to the Sessions page.

9. **Design review sign-off.** Before implementation begins, a short design note (one page or less) in `docs/helix/02-design/` captures the Workers-vs-Sessions mental model, the chosen controls, and any CLI primitives that need to exist. Implementation PR links to that note.","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:18:04.067488813Z","description":"## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../workers` renders a table of workers but has no buttons, no controls, and no explanation of how it differs from `/sessions`. The layout (`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte`) shows ID / Kind / State / Current Bead / Attempts — read-only. Clicking a row drills into live-streaming logs and recent events, but offers no actions there either.

This raised a fair question: **what is this screen for, and how is it different from Sessions?** The answer isn't in the UI.

## Ground truth (from the CLI)

- **Worker** = a persistent execute-loop process draining the bead queue (`ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-loop`). Has a lifecycle: running / idle / stopped / error. Has a *current bead* and cumulative attempts/successes/failures. Can be stopped via `ddx agent workers stop`. Long-lived.
- **Session** = a historical record of one agent invocation (harness, model, bead, tokens, cost, outcome, start/end). Immutable after completion. One worker produces many sessions over its lifetime.

So: Workers are *processes you operate*. Sessions are *events you review*. The current UI exposes both as flat tables and lets the reader guess which is which.

## Gaps

1. **No disambiguation.** No page-level copy, no link between the two, no worker→sessions navigation ("show sessions produced by this worker"), no session→worker backlink.
2. **No lifecycle controls on Workers.** The CLI has `ddx agent workers stop` and `ddx work` (start). The UI has neither button. No stop, start, restart, or "cancel current bead" affordance. The State column is a spectator sport.
3. **No worker start UX.** There is no way to start a queue drain from the UI — users must drop to a terminal.
4. **Unclear empty state.** "No workers found." does not say whether that is normal (nothing running) or broken (server can't see workers). No hint about how to start one.
5. **No worker details beyond logs.** Worker detail page shows live phase + log tail + tool calls but not: worker config (project path, profile/harness, started-at, last-heartbeat), resource usage, or the list of sessions this worker has produced.

## Proposed direction

Split into two deliverables — do them together if we're touching this screen anyway, but they are independently landable:

### A. Information architecture (copy + navigation)

- Add a short header description to each page: "Workers drain the bead queue. Sessions are the history of what they ran."
- Mutual links: Workers page links to "Recent sessions →", Sessions page links to "Workers →".
- On worker detail: a "Sessions" tab or section listing sessions where `session.workerId == this.id` (requires session records to carry workerID; check whether they do).
- On session detail (expandable row already exists): if the session has a workerID, surface it as a link back to the worker.

### B. Lifecycle controls

- **Stop** button per running worker row → calls a GraphQL mutation that invokes the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop <id>`. Confirms before acting.
- **Start worker** button in the page header → opens a small form (project, profile/harness, effort, optional bead filter) and calls a `startWorker` mutation that shells out to `ddx work` in the background. The form should default to sane values and be submittable in one click for the common case.
- **Cancel current bead** action on the worker detail page — signals the worker to abandon the in-flight bead without killing the loop. (Only if CLI primitive exists; otherwise descope and note in the bead.)
- All mutations must be idempotent and log an audit record viewable in the UI (who did it, when). Reuse existing session-event plumbing if possible.

### Out of scope

- Rewriting execute-loop semantics.
- Cross-project worker orchestration.
- Auth / permissions model for who can stop workers (assume single-operator for now; file a follow-up if multi-operator becomes real).","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"omlx-vidar-1235","resolved_model":"qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:00:24.797884238Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=omlx-vidar-1235 model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"tier=cheap harness=agent model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b probe=ok

241:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer reviewing what my agents have done, I open the Executions view, filter to a specific bead or verdict or harness, click into one, and see the prompt it received, the verdict it produced, the session it corresponds to (with cost + tokens), and every tool call it made — all without leaving the browser.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Sidebar entry.** "Executions" added to the project sidebar between "Sessions" and "Personas" (or wherever fits the existing visual order — design call; captured in the PR description). Active-highlight works.

2. **List route.** `/executions` renders a paginated, filterable table with columns: timestamp, bead (id + title as link), harness/model, verdict (color-coded pass/block/error/other), duration, cost. Default sort: newest first. Filters: verdict, harness, bead, since/until date, free-text search over prompt first ~200 chars.

3. **Detail route.** `/executions/<id>` shows five tabs as described (Manifest, Prompt, Result, Session, Tool calls). Tab switching doesn't re-fetch already-loaded data.

4. **Tool calls pane.** Virtualized list. First 50 calls load with the page; subsequent pages load on scroll. Each entry shows tool name + collapsed input/output, expandable on click. Links to the raw agent-log file for operators who want to diff.

5. **GraphQL completeness.** `executions`, `execution`, `executionToolCalls` resolvers exist and pass feat-008-style integration tests. Schema documented in `schema.graphql`.

6. **Cross-links present.**
   - Bead detail renders an "Executions" section listing linked executions with verdict + timestamp, each clicking into the detail route.
   - Session detail shows "Execution" link when the session has one.
   - Commits page shows execution link when commit sha == execution.result_rev.

7. **Fixture + perf.**
   - Seeded fixture: ≥1,000 executions, ≥100 with seeded tool-call streams (≥50 calls each).
   - List view on this fixture: p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP.
   - Detail view (no tool calls): p95 ≤ 150ms HTTP.
   - Tool-call page 1 (first 50 entries): p95 ≤ 300ms HTTP.
   - If any target cannot be met in-place, raise DB-substrate decision with user before switching.

8. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds a project with 3 executions across 2 beads with mixed verdicts.
   - Navigates to `/executions`, asserts the table renders 3 rows and filter-by-verdict narrows correctly.
   - Clicks into one execution, asserts all 5 tabs exist and the Prompt tab shows the seeded prompt.
   - Opens the Tool calls tab, expands an entry, asserts input/output rendering.
   - Clicks the linked bead from the detail, asserts navigation to bead detail and that the Executions section on that bead lists this execution.

9. **Depends on.**
   - Soft dependency on `ddx-2ceb02fa` for the Session tab's cost/token numbers. If not yet landed, render "not available" for those fields and wire them in after.
   - Adjacent: `ddx-6904a90b` (billing mode) — Session tab's cost shows the correct bucket once that lands.","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:55:34.427486388Z","description":"## Observed / Motivation

`.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/{manifest.json, prompt.md, result.json}` bundles are already being produced per `ddx agent execute-bead` attempt — 469 of them on the current repo. They contain the canonical record of what an agent was asked to do and what verdict the attempt produced. **Nothing in the web UI surfaces them today.** A user who wants to ask "why did this bead end up in this state?" has to drop to the filesystem.

Sessions (`sessions.jsonl` / sharded index per `ddx-2ceb02fa`) carry the harness/cost/token telemetry but not the verdict and not the bead linkage. Agent log streams (`agent-*.jsonl`) carry the tool-call trace but are scattered across hundreds of files. **Executions are the natural join** — they connect a bead, a session, a prompt, a verdict, and a tool-call stream.

## Ground truth

Per-execution on disk:

```
.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/
  manifest.json      { harness, model, base_rev, result_rev, verdict, bead_id, execution_dir }
  prompt.md          the prompt that was sent
  result.json        { verdict, rationale, ... }
```

Relations:
- `manifest.bead_id` → bead
- a session id is recorded in bead events (`kind:cost`, `kind:routing`) and in the agent-log stream; both can map back to an execution via correlation metadata
- tool calls live in `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` (and `agent-s-*.jsonl`), keyed by session id

## Scope

### Part 1 — GraphQL surface

- `executions(projectId, first, after, beadId?, since?, until?, verdict?, harness?)` — paginated list of execution bundles.
- `execution(id)` — one bundle, fully materialized: manifest fields, prompt markdown, result JSON, linked bead (id + title), linked session (id + harness + model + cost + token totals), tool-call stream pointer.
- `executionToolCalls(id, first, after)` — paginated tool-call stream for the execution, read lazily from the agent-log file. Matches the event shape used by the workers detail page.

Executions source of truth: directory scan of `.ddx/executions/` filtered/paginated at the resolver. Shard discipline: follow the sessions-index monthly shard pattern (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) if listing all executions grows beyond tens of thousands — per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`, raise the storage decision with the user before switching substrate.

### Part 2 — Sidebar + routes

- Add "Executions" to the project sidebar (`NavShell.svelte`).
- Routes:
  - `/nodes/<n>/projects/<p>/executions` — list view with filters (verdict, harness, bead, date range, free-text over prompt). Table columns: timestamp, bead, harness/model, verdict (pass/block/error), duration, cost.
  - `/nodes/<n>/projects/<p>/executions/<id>` — detail view with five tabs: **Manifest**, **Prompt** (rendered markdown), **Result** (rationale + raw JSON toggle), **Session** (harness, model, cost, token totals, link to `/sessions/<id>`), **Tool calls** (virtualized lazy-scroll list of tool_use + tool_result pairs with expandable payloads).

### Part 3 — Cross-links (make the graph real)

- **Bead detail:** new "Executions" section listing all executions for this bead with verdict + timestamp + link. Already have data: manifest.bead_id.
- **Session detail:** new "Execution" link pointing to the execution this session ran for, when the session has a linked execution.
- **Commits page:** already shows commits; if a commit was produced by an execution (result_rev == commit sha), surface the execution link.
- **Graph/Docs pages:** out of scope for this bead.

### Part 4 — Perf

- Fixture: ≥1,000 executions, per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`. Tool-call streams seeded for 10% of them.
- Targets: list view p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP, detail view without tool calls p95 ≤ 150ms HTTP, tool-call page-1 (first 50) p95 ≤ 300ms HTTP.
- If these can't be met with filesystem scan + in-memory filter, raise DB-substrate decision with user per standing directive.

## Out of scope

- Writing new execution bundles (that's already happening).
- Executions from other projects/nodes (keep per-project for v1).
- Tool-call payload redaction / privacy. File a follow-up if relevant.

2. Repro fixture bead-closed-no-evidence.jsonl (from ddx-004eccbc) replays through the execute-loop close path and is rejected — the bead does not transition to closed.
3. Unit test covers the three terminal failure modes: reviewer exit non-zero, reviewer output empty, reviewer output unparseable.
4. No new invariant machinery here — the closure invariant bead (ddx-e30e60a9) owns cross-path enforcement; this bead owns the execute-loop symptom repro.","created_at":"2026-04-20T13:56:00.206769663Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-738edf47","depends_on_id":"ddx-e30e60a9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T14:03:56Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-738edf47","depends_on_id":"ddx-004eccbc","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T14:04:11Z"}],"description":"Reporter: nexiq+axon 2026-04-18/19/20. Symptom 3 of review-malfunction report. Most damaging of the three report findings because it hides incomplete work.

Observed on axon `axon-c5cc071a` (RBAC field/row/entity spec, ~2000-word AC) 2026-04-19:

```json
{
  "id": "axon-c5cc071a",
  "claimed-at": "2026-04-19T23:20:45Z",
  "claimed-machine": "sindri",
  "claimed-pid": "3479503",
  "execute-loop-heartbeat-at": "2026-04-19T23:20:45.364638776Z",
  "updated_at": "2026-04-19T23:25:18.98959532Z",
  "status": "closed",
  "events": []
}
```

4m33s from claim to closed, `events: []`, no execution bundle under `.ddx/executions/` referencing the bead, no git commit referencing the id, no branch, no notes. Reopened 2026-04-20 as `axon-c5cc071a`. Evidence this is worker-side: no other actor claimed the bead, `claimed-pid` matches execute-loop PID pattern, `execute-loop-heartbeat-at` was set at claim — a manual `ddx bead update` would not leave `events: []` while setting `status: closed`.

Root-cause hypothesis from report: the worker's cleanup path closes the bead on primary-task success even when the reviewer sub-step errors out without returning a parseable verdict (e.g. context-overflow `n_keep: 1,868,459 >= n_ctx: 196,608` seen in reporter's LLM server logs, or any exception in the review state-machine path). A `bead.result` event that never gets emitted still leaves the bead closed if the exit path is not gated on verdict presence.

Closure contract should be: a bead transitions to `status=closed` only when a valid terminal review verdict (APPROVE / REQUEST_CHANGES / BLOCK / SKIP) is recorded alongside an execution bundle and at least one bead event. Worker crash, malformed reviewer output, or context-overflow during review must transition the bead back to `in_progress` with a retry marker — never to `closed`.

Prior related bead `ddx-b57be3ab` (closed) addressed BLOCK-with-empty-rationale but treated the rationale-missing case as `review_malfunction` without closing; this report shows a separate path where the closure itself happens despite reviewer failure.","id":"ddx-738edf47","issue_type":"bug","labels":["area:agent","area:exec","area:bead","kind:bug","kind:correctness","goal:throughput-per-dollar","wave:2","workstream:reviewer"],"priority":1,"status":"closed","title":"exec: bead closes without valid reviewer verdict — silent false-closure hides unfinished work","updated_at":"2026-04-20T16:23:03.738432327Z"}

328:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer using the web UI on a real-world-sized workspace (tens of projects × hundreds of beads each), the beads list opens in under a second and the cross-project all-beads view stays usable. Before any perf fix lands, we can reproduce the problem and measure improvement — not guess.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Perf harness exists and is reusable.**
   - Fixture builder generates `N × M` beads across projects with configurable shape (status mix, label density, dep count). Reuses the real `bead.Store` write path (no fake writer).
   - Benchmark covers at minimum: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject`, `beads` (cross-project), `docGraph`, `sessions`. Adding a new target is a single-table-row change.
   - Each target produces p50/p95/p99 for in-process calls and end-to-end HTTP. Results write to a human-readable report (markdown or JSON) plus a machine-readable artifact suitable for CI diffing later.

2. **Baseline recorded.** First run of the harness on the current `main` binary is stored in the bead notes and in `docs/helix/04-observe/perf/YYYY-MM-DD-baseline.md` (or equivalent existing location). Numbers include hardware + go version footer.

3. **BeadsByProject scope fix.** `BeadsByProject` calls a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, …)` that opens exactly one project's store. Unit test asserts only that project's store is opened (no iteration over other projects).

4. **Filter pushdown.** Status/label/projectID filters apply at the per-file / per-entry parse boundary, not post-materialization. Benchmark captures before/after for the common filtered case (e.g., `status=open`).

5. **Perf targets on fixture (10 projects × 500 beads = 5000 beads total):**
   - `bead(id:)` p95 ≤ 50ms in-process / ≤ 200ms HTTP (already covered by `ddx-ad0db8fd`; this bead may inherit the work).
   - `beadsByProject(first=50)` p95 ≤ 75ms in-process / ≤ 250ms HTTP.
   - `beads(first=50, no projectID)` p95 ≤ 200ms in-process / ≤ 500ms HTTP.
   These are targets for the **first** pass (scope + pushdown). If they are not met with (1)+(2), escalate to the in-memory index (step 3 of Part 2 in the description) and update these targets with the new baseline.

6. **Stale-read safety.** Regardless of fix chosen, a test writes a bead via `ddx bead update`, then queries it via GraphQL, then asserts the new value is visible on the very next request. Works under concurrency: N concurrent readers + writers interleaved without torn reads or panics.

7. **CI posture (soft).** The harness is runnable via `make bench-graphql` (or the project's equivalent). CI wiring to fail on >25% regression is a follow-up bead — recorded in this bead's notes with the proposed threshold but not implemented here.

8. **Playwright smoke.** Opening `/beads` on the fixture project returns an interactive (clickable) list within 1s. Opening the cross-project `/beads` page returns within 2s. These are ceilings, not p95 targets — guards against outright regression, not fine-grained perf.

9. **Cross-reference.** This bead's notes list `ddx-ad0db8fd` (bead detail) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions shard/index) as the other two bead/session perf threads that should inherit this harness.","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:41:52.271330549Z","description":"## Observed

Per-project `/beads` page is noticeably slow; the cross-project "all beads" view is substantially slower. Related: `ddx-ad0db8fd` already identified the single-bead-detail slowness caused by the same storage pattern.

## Root cause (same pattern, different call site)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_beads.go:22,40` —

- `Beads` (cross-project) and `BeadsByProject` (single-project) both call `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots(status, label, projectID, search)`.
- `GetBeadSnapshots` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105`) iterates every registered project, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), filters in Go. Cost is `O(projects × beads_per_project)` on every call, independent of the filter/page size.
- All filtering (status, label, project, search) is Go-side, post-read. The page-size window is applied in `beadConnectionFromSnapshots` after the full corpus is already materialized.
- Cross-project "all beads" = same loop, just without the projectID filter. It is the worst case by design.

This is the same shape that's breaking the Sessions feed (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) and single-bead detail (`ddx-ad0db8fd`). A point fix here is insufficient — we need a measurement surface first so we stop relying on anecdote.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Perf harness (foundational; enables everything else)

Introduce a reusable GraphQL perf harness under `cli/internal/server/perf/` or `cli/internal/server/graphql/bench_test.go`:

- Seeded fixture builder: generates N projects × M beads with configurable distribution of status/labels/dependencies. Reuses existing store primitives (no fake data layer).
- Go benchmark + scriptable HTTP runner (wrapper around an already-running server) reporting p50/p95/p99 per query shape.
- Targets covered on day one: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject(projectID, first, after, status, label, search)`, `beads(projectID=nil, first, after, status, label)`, `docGraph`, `sessions` (so the sessions shard work `ddx-2ceb02fa` inherits this harness). New query targets are added by writing a single table row.
- Thresholds are **recorded, not asserted**, in the first pass. CI surfaces a regression if a threshold is breached beyond a configured delta (e.g., +25% p95). Initial pass is "land the harness and record baseline"; a follow-up wires it into CI.

### Part 2 — Fix the scan pattern

Minimum viable, in priority order:

1. **Scope by project when the query is scoped.** `BeadsByProject` should call a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, filters…)` that only opens that one project's store. Trivial change; eliminates the cross-project factor for the common case.
2. **Early filter pushdown.** `GetBeadSnapshots` should apply status/label/projectID filters at the file-parse boundary where cheap, not after materializing every bead. (Still reads every file — deeper fix below.)
3. **Lightweight in-memory index with invalidation.** After (1) and (2), if "all beads" or search is still slow, introduce an index keyed `{projectID, beadID}` with basic metadata (status, labels, title, updatedAt). Refresh on bead write + debounced filesystem watch. Full-body reads stay lazy — the index never holds bodies. This is the same storage discipline we adopted for sessions in `ddx-2ceb02fa`.

The harness from Part 1 is the gate for choosing between (2) and (3); don't commit to the index without numbers.

## Out of scope

- Full-text search infrastructure (tracked separately if needed).
- Changing the on-disk bead format.
- Cross-node federation performance. Current concern is a single server reading a single workspace.","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"omlx-vidar-1235","resolved_model":"qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:59:28.485694569Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=omlx-vidar-1235 model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"tier=cheap harness=agent model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b probe=ok

base_rev=892c05a155255ec4d96b108a71e9f4171c419ef8","created_at":"2026-04-23T01:08:26.762659611Z","kind":"execute-bead","source":"ddx agent execute-loop","summary":"land_conflict"}],"execute-loop-last-detail":"escalation exhausted: unsupported model "minimax/minimax-m2.7" for harness "gemini"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","feature":"FEAT-008","id":"ddx-9ce6842a","issue_type":"task","labels":["feat-008","feat-004","perf","graphql"],"notes":"## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.
**AC §9 — Bead notes cross-reference**: The acceptance criteria require this bead's notes to list `ddx-ad0db8fd` and `ddx-2ceb02fa`. The baseline `.md` and code comments mention them, but no `ddx bead update` call or notes update appears in the diff. If the notes were updated via a separate mechanism not captured in this branch, this finding can be dismissed.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline and perf-thread cross-reference

Baseline recorded in `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and JSON sibling from the 10 projects x 500 beads fixture (5,000 beads total, 1,000 session rows). Environment: `go=go1.26.2 goos=linux goarch=arm64 cpu-cores=12`.

Key p95 results: `bead` 6.72ms in-process / 6.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 8.84ms in-process / 9.29ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.16ms in-process / 75.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.45ms in-process / 6.87ms HTTP.

Cross-reference for inherited harness work: `ddx-ad0db8fd` covers single-bead detail lookup; `ddx-2ceb02fa` covers sessions shard/index work. Both should use/extend this GraphQL perf harness.

Review follow-up: AC §8 cross-project Playwright smoke now navigates to `/nodes/{nodeId}/beads` and exercises the `beads` GraphQL query instead of the project-scoped `beadsByProject` path. Proposed CI posture remains a follow-up +25% p95 regression gate, not implemented in this bead.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline refresh after session-index fixture alignment

Re-ran `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` after updating the perf fixture to seed current pointer-only session-index shards instead of legacy `sessions.jsonl`. Refreshed artifacts: `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and `.json`.

Refreshed p95 results: `bead` 7.23ms in-process / 7.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 7.18ms in-process / 7.83ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.49ms in-process / 67.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.74ms in-process / 7.73ms HTTP; `agentSessions` 13.45ms in-process / 14.19ms HTTP.

## 2026-04-23 — Final `make bench-graphql` verification

After fixing `cli/Makefile` to pass an absolute `DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DIR`, `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` writes to the intended repo docs path. Final p95 results from that run: `bead` 6.76ms in-process / 7.20ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 6.88ms in-process / 7.85ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 63.22ms in-process / 71.82ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.75ms in-process / 7.56ms HTTP; `agentSessions` 13.24ms in-process / 13.64ms HTTP.","priority":1,"status":"open","title":"Bead list and cross-project all-beads are too slow: establish a GraphQL perf harness and fix N·M scan pattern","updated_at":"2026-04-23T01:08:26.800066402Z"}
330:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer looking at the personas UI, I want to understand at a glance what personas are, where they come from (library vs project), and I want to fork or create a project-local persona without leaving the UI. Library content stays read-only so I cannot accidentally mutate shared content; project content is fully editable.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Design note + policy decision recorded.** Before implementation, a one-page note in `docs/helix/02-design/` captures D1 (library vs project policy, override rules, file locations) and D2 (scoped UI additions). Implementation PR links to it.

2. **Project-local persona directory.** `.ddx/personas/*.md` is a recognized location. `ddx persona --list` shows both library and project personas with a `source` tag. Override rule: a project persona with the same name as a library persona wins for that project's bindings; unit test covers this precedence.

3. **GraphQL additions.** New mutations: `personaCreate(name, body, projectId) → Persona`, `personaUpdate(name, body, projectId) → Persona`, `personaDelete(name, projectId) → {ok}`. All gated on `source == "project"`; attempting any of them on a library persona returns a typed error. Each mutation persists to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` under the given project.

4. **CLI parity.** `ddx persona new`, `edit`, `fork`, `delete` exist and share the same underlying operations as the GraphQL mutations (no duplicated logic). Integration test asserts that a `ddx persona new` followed by a GraphQL `personas` query returns the new persona.

5. **UI — explainer copy.** The personas page shows a one-sentence description of what personas are and a second sentence distinguishing library vs project sources. Playwright asserts both sentences are present.

6. **UI — source badge.** Each persona row shows a `library` or `project` badge. Playwright seeds one of each and asserts correct badges.

7. **UI — New / Edit / Delete for project personas.** A "New persona" button opens a form with name + body fields. Project-source rows have Edit and Delete affordances. Library-source rows have neither. Playwright creates a persona, edits it, verifies the edit persisted, then deletes it.

8. **UI — Fork library to project.** Library rows have a "Fork to project" action. Clicking it prompts for a name (default: library name with `-local` suffix on collision), creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md`, and navigates to the editor. Playwright asserts the forked file exists and opens in the editor.

9. **No regressions.** Existing list/show/bind flows still work. Playwright smoke of the pre-existing binding UX passes.

10. **Empty project-local state.** When a project has zero project-local personas, the UI shows a subtle hint: "No project personas yet. Fork a library persona or create a new one." Playwright asserts.","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:30:40.66472156Z","description":"## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../personas` is a well-crafted browser + bind UX, but it is read-only: no add, no edit, no delete, no explanation of what the reader is looking at or why they cannot change it. A user looking at it can reasonably ask "what is this for?" and "why can't I change anything?" and the UI answers neither.

## Ground truth

- **What a persona is.** A markdown file with frontmatter under `library/personas/*.md` defining AI personality, expertise, principles, communication style. Shipped defaults: architect, code-reviewer, implementer, specification-enforcer, test-engineer.
- **How it's used.** Projects declare role→persona bindings in `.ddx/config.yaml` via `persona_bindings`. When an agent performs role X, the bound persona's body is injected into its system prompt.
- **What the tool exposes today.**
  - CLI: `ddx persona --list`, `--show <name>`, `--bind <persona> --role <role>`. No create/edit/delete.
  - GraphQL: `personaBind` mutation only (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:150`). No `personaCreate/Update/Delete` in schema.
  - UI: list/show/bind parity with CLI.
- **Why it evolved this way.** Personas have been treated as *library content* — authored carefully, version-controlled upstream, pulled into projects via `ddx update` and contributed back via `ddx contribute`. The tool deliberately did not become a persona editor.

## The gap

The library-only model doesn't hold up for real use:

1. **Project-local personas are a real need.** Teams want "our code-reviewer, with our opinions about our codebase." Today they either (a) fork the persona file and push upstream (wrong — it's not generally useful), (b) never customize (bad outcomes), or (c) drop a file into some ad-hoc local path and hope it works.
2. **The UI implies capability it doesn't have.** A list + detail view with no hint about source policy reads as "unfinished feature" rather than "intentional read-only".
3. **Binding alone isn't enough.** Even if editing stays out of scope, users need to understand *which personas are available*, *where they come from* (library vs project-local), and *how to change that*.

## Proposed direction

Two decisions (pick before implementation):

### D1 — Policy: who owns persona content?

Recommend: **both, with a clear boundary.**
- Library personas: `library/personas/*.md` — read-only in UI, contribute upstream via `ddx contribute`.
- Project-local personas: new directory `.ddx/personas/*.md` — fully editable in UI, live with the project, not in the shared library. Override semantics: a project-local persona with the same name as a library persona takes precedence for that project's bindings.

### D2 — Scope of the UI

Minimum viable, given D1:
- **Explainer copy** on the personas page header: "Personas are AI personality templates. Library personas are shared; project personas live with this project. Bind a persona to a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`."
- **Source badge** on each persona row: `library` or `project`.
- **Project-local editor.** For project personas:
  - `New persona` button → form (name, roles/tags, body markdown). Creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` with frontmatter.
  - `Edit` on project rows.
  - `Delete` on project rows with confirm.
- **Library rows stay read-only.** Action on a library row: `Fork to project` — copies the library file to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` (with a default rename suggestion on collision) and opens the editor. No in-place edit, no delete.
- **Bind affordance remains.** Binding UI continues to work for both sources.

## What this bead does NOT decide

- Multi-project persona sharing (e.g., org-level personas between library and project). File a follow-up if it becomes pressing.
- Rich editor affordances beyond a plain markdown textarea with preview. Frontmatter can be a YAML-ish textarea for v1.
- Syncing project personas upstream — that remains `ddx contribute` and is out of scope.
- Any change to how personas are injected into agent prompts (the read path on the server).

## CLI parity

Whatever the UI gains, the CLI gains too. Add:
- `ddx persona new <name>` — creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` scaffold.
- `ddx persona edit <name>` — opens `$EDITOR` on the project-local persona (errors on library).
- `ddx persona fork <library-name> [--as <new-name>]` — copies library → project.
- `ddx persona delete <name>` — project-local only.

356:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer browsing the web UI beads view, I want clicking a bead to open its detail pane within ~200ms on a repo with hundreds of beads across several projects, so the UI feels interactive.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. A benchmark/harness exists (Go benchmark or scripted GraphQL client) that measures end-to-end `bead(id:)` latency against a fixture repo with ≥ 5 projects and ≥ 500 beads total. Output is a p50/p95 number, not a pass/fail bool.
2. Baseline is recorded in the bead notes (actual number measured on the current `main` binary at the time of fix).
3. After the fix, p95 for `bead(id:)` on the fixture is ≤ 50ms in-process / ≤ 200ms over HTTP on a dev laptop.
4. The fix does not introduce a stale-read regression: writing a bead via `ddx bead update` and then fetching it via GraphQL returns the new value on the next request (verified by a test that does write-then-read).
5. Concurrency-safe: an integration test spawns N concurrent `bead(id:)` queries interleaved with `bead update` mutations and asserts no panics, no torn reads, and consistent responses.
6. Playwright e2e: open `/nodes/.../beads`, click a bead row, assert the sidepane shows the bead title within 1s (generous budget; guards against regressions not the p95).","closing_commit_sha":"c1822352b4bbfa4181f96a203bce522d249f342d","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:07:18.806879855Z","description":"## Observed

Opening a bead in the sidepane on `/nodes/.../projects/.../beads?q=test` takes 5+ seconds. The beads list renders quickly; the delay is on single-bead lookup.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver.go:93-100` — the `Bead(id)` resolver calls `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots("", "", "", "")` with no filters, which at `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105` walks **every project**, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (full directory scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), then iterates the result in Go to find one matching ID.

So every sidepane open = `O(projects × beads_per_project)` disk reads + parses, for a lookup that should be `O(1)` or `O(log n)`.

`bead.Store` already exposes `Get(id)` (`cli/internal/bead/store.go:450`), but the resolver doesn't use it — nor does it know which project the bead lives in, because the GraphQL schema exposes `bead(id:)` without a project scope.

## Proposed direction (not prescriptive)

Any of these would resolve it; pick based on measurements:

1. **Project scope the query.** Change schema to `bead(projectId:, id:)` (or accept a composite global id) and call `Store.Get` directly.
2. **Maintain an in-memory index** `beadID → projectID` refreshed on bead mutations and on a debounced filesystem watcher; resolver does one map lookup + one `Store.Get`.
3. **Short-TTL snapshot cache** behind `GetBeadSnapshots`, invalidated on bead writes.

Before picking: add a baseline measurement harness (see acceptance) so we know the actual p50/p95 before and after, not just anecdote.","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"omlx-vidar-1235","resolved_model":"qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:59:05.950521722Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=omlx-vidar-1235 model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"tier=cheap harness=agent model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b probe=ok

375:{"acceptance":"DDx updates to the released ddx-agent v0.2.0 artifact/module reference after the upstream release completes; DDx verification passes against that released version; and any DDx-side compatibility fixes or follow-up beads are recorded if the upgrade reveals integration drift","closing_commit_sha":"d7a3ba1","created_at":"2026-04-10T17:34:31.734611314Z","description":"<context-digest>

Update DDx to consume ddx-agent v0.2.0 once the upstream release is complete.

## Why this update exists
DDx embeds or invokes ddx-agent and should move forward to the next released minor version after the agent release is published cleanly. This bead is intentionally queued now so the DDx-side adoption work is ready as soon as the upstream release is available.

## Upstream changes since the prior DDx adoption point
- acceptance-test coverage was expanded substantially across CLI, runtime, provider, routing, updater, installer, telemetry, and integration surfaces
- CI/build gates were hardened and unified: lint, gosec, govulncheck, format, normal tests, and race tests now share one canonical repo-local/CI command surface
- repo-local lefthook pre-push gates were added so DDx contributors working on ddx-agent can run the same checks locally before push
- Go toolchain pin moved to 1.26.2 to pick up standard-library security fixes required by CI vulnerability scanning
- provider JSON/config output redaction was tightened for CLI security-scan compliance
- telemetry, retry ownership, session continuity, usage/reporting, and related runtime work landed earlier in the same release train and are now part of the released minor version

## DDx work expected
- update the ddx-agent dependency/version pin to the released v0.2.0 artifact or module reference used by DDx
- run DDx integration and acceptance checks against the released version, not a local SHA
- verify that DDx expectations around CLI commands, output formats, usage reporting, and agent-session handling still match upstream behavior
- verify that any DDx automation or docs referencing ddx-agent setup mention the new Go/tooling and hook expectations only if DDx vendors or contributes directly in that repo; do not leak agent-repo-only setup into DDx user docs unless DDx actually depends on it
- capture any DDx-side compatibility fixes separately if the upgrade exposes integration drift

## Notes
Do not start this work until ddx-agent v0.2.0 is actually released and visible as a completed upstream release.","id":"ddx-b7df7517","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","area:agent","area:deps","kind:integration"],"priority":1,"status":"closed","title":"Update DDx to ddx-agent v0.2.0","updated_at":"2026-04-10T19:05:09.702519157Z"}

2. cli/internal/agent/omlx_e2e_test.go runs a ddx work execute-bead orchestration against a test-double provider that replays the fixtures, asserting: no 'unexpected end of JSON input', final outcome matches fixture expectation, bead state mutates correctly.

3. Test runs in CI without requiring a live omlx endpoint.

4. Opt-in live smoke test (//go:build omlx_smoke) covers the same happy-path scenario against a real vidar-omlx when invoked manually.

README or docs/ entry describes how to regenerate fixtures via scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh when omlx server behavior changes.","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T04:14:07Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"ad14ee84ff0faa5b8754f5274ea795fb1511cde7","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:01:36.207143767Z","description":"## Context

ddx-agent v0.3.14 shipped the SSE comment-frame filter that unblocks omlx. Manual dogfooding (2026-04-18) confirmed a single bead executing via vidar-omlx for 6+ minutes with 17,000+ streaming events before the fix caused the regression this bead is meant to prevent: we have no automated assertion that the omlx path actually stays healthy.

Both fresh-eyes reviews (codex + opus, 2026-04-18) flagged: 'missing bead for v0.3.14 consumer-side smoke test in DDx'. This is that bead.

## Fix

Add an end-to-end smoke test that runs a real ddx work invocation against a recorded-or-live omlx endpoint and asserts the attempt closes (or at least produces a closing-status result: success / already_satisfied / a genuine model-side error) without surfacing 'unexpected end of JSON input' or silent stream termination.

Two implementation options:

1. **Record + replay via virtual harness** (preferred for CI isolation). Capture a full AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE_STREAM_FULL=1 trace of a successful vidar-omlx run, store it as a fixture, replay via a test double that emulates omlx's SSE quirks (keep-alive comment frames, reasoning warmup silence, reasoning_content deltas). Test asserts ddx-agent SDK consumes the full stream, ddx work's execute-bead orchestrator records closing status, and bead state mutates correctly.

2. **Live smoke, opt-in** (developer-machine only). A test file guarded by build tag  that makes a real request to vidar-omlx. Useful for catching provider-side regressions (e.g., omlx server ships a breaking change). Skip in CI.

Do BOTH: option 1 for CI signal, option 2 for manual release verification.

## What this prevents

- Regression: a future ddx-agent bump reintroduces the SSE-comment-frame bug or similar streaming defect. CI fixture test catches it before the bump merges.
- Regression: DDx-side change breaks the omlx dispatch path (e.g., adds a header that trips omlx's parser). CI catches it.
- Silent-success: ddx work completes but actually landed a bogus result_rev because of a subtle SSE-frame-dropping bug. Fixture replay asserts the expected final outcome.

## Coverage target

Minimum: one happy-path fixture (successful bead close) AND one error-path fixture (model returns malformed tool-call; execute-bead records task_failed cleanly without corrupting the bead tracker).

## Files likely touched

- cli/internal/agent/omlx_e2e_test.go (new) — fixture replay + assertions
- testdata/omlx-wire/ (new) — captured AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE JSONL fixtures
- scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh (new) — helper to regenerate fixtures against a live endpoint

## Depends on

- agent-941e7e42 (AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE_STREAM_FULL=1) shipped in v0.3.14 — needed to capture full the consumer-side\n+end-to-end smoke test that guards `ddx work` against regressions on the\n+`ddx-agent → vidar-omlx` dispatch path. Context: bead **ddx-bbb2d177**, which\n+was filed after v0.3.14 shipped the `sseCommentFilter` fix for bead\n+**agent-f237e07b** (upstream).\n+\n+## File format\n+\n+Each fixture is a JSONL file with two record kinds:\n+\n+- First line: `{\"kind\":\"meta\", ...}` — describes the capture and the assertion\n+  shape the replay test will enforce (expected status, substrings that must or\n+  must not appear in the assembled content / error).\n+- Remaining lines: `{\"kind\":\"frame\",\"body\":\"<raw SSE body>\"}` — one frame per\n+  record, written verbatim (including `\\n\\n` terminators) to the test HTTP\n+  response in arrival order.\n+\n+Frames include both `: keep-alive` SSE comment frames (the exact shape that\n+triggered the v0.3.13 \"unexpected end of JSON input\" regression) and normal\n+`data: {...}` chunks.\n+\n+## When to regenerate\n+\n+Regenerate fixtures only when real omlx server behavior changes in a way the\n+existing captures no longer represent — for example, a new reasoning-phase\n+frame shape, a header shift, or a chunk-boundary change. Do NOT regenerate\n+just to refresh timestamps.\n+\n+## How to regenerate\n+\n+Use `scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh`, which wraps `ddx agent run` with\n+`AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE_STREAM_FULL=1` (shipped in ddx-agent v0.3.14) so the full\n+stream is captured to a sidecar JSONL, then reshapes it into the fixture\n+format:\n+\n+```bash\n+scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh \\\n+  --endpoint http://vidar:1235/v1 \\\n+  --model    Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit \\\n+  --prompt   \"Reply with the word ready.\" \\\n+  --name     happy-path \\\n+  --status   success \\\n+  --out      cli/internal/agent/testdata/omlx-wire/happy_path.jsonl\n+```\n+\n+After the script writes the fixture, hand-edit the `meta.expected` block to\n+pin the `content_contains` / `error_contains` / `finish_reason` assertions for\n+the capture you just took — the script cannot infer those from the wire\n+bytes alone.\n+\n+## Why fixtures live here instead of the upstream agent repo\n+\n+The agent repo (`DocumentDrivenDX/agent`) has its own provider-level SSE\n+tests in `provider/openai/openai_test.go`. Those guard the SDK's handling of\n+SSE framing in isolation. The fixtures in *this* directory guard the DDx\n+consumer — `ddx work` → `Runner.RunAgent` → `agentlib.Run` — against both\n+SDK regressions (a bad ddx-agent bump) and DDx-side regressions (a header or\n+request-shape change that trips omlx's parser).
